title: Confidence interval and standard error of the mean
body_html:
Confidence Intervals (CI)

The confidence interval is a key concept in medical statistics and is often misunderstood.

Formal definition:
A confidence interval is a range of values for a population parameter (such as the mean or proportion) constructed so that this range has a specified probability of including the true value of that parameter. This specified probability is called the confidence level, and the ends of the interval are called the confidence limits.

Plain English:
A confidence interval gives a range within which the true value (e.g. the true mean or effect size) is likely to lie, based on your sample data. A 95% confidence level means that, if you repeated your study many times, 95% of the calculated intervals would contain the true population value.

Why are confidence intervals useful?
  • They help quantify the precision of an estimate (e.g. mean difference, risk ratio).
  • A narrow interval indicates greater precision, whilst a wider interval indicates more uncertainty.
  • They help assess statistical significance:
  • If the confidence interval for a mean difference includes 0, the result is not statistically significant. For example, a confidence interval of –2 to 10 mmHg includes 0 and is therefore not statistically significant.
  • If the confidence interval for a ratio (e.g. relative risk, odds ratio) includes 1 the result is not statistically significant
  • They aid clinical interpretation: the range of values provides context on how meaningful the result might be.

How is a confidence interval calculated?
Confidence intervals are built around the sample mean using the standard error of the mean (SEM).

Calculating SEM:
To calculate SEM we can use the equation below:

SEM = s / √n

Where s = sample standard deviation, n = sample size

SEM reflects how much sample means vary from the true population mean on repeated sampling. As sample size increases, SEM decreases & confidence intervals become narrower and more precise

To calculate a 95% confidence interval for the mean:
  • Lower limit = mean − (1.96 × SEM)
  • Upper limit = mean + (1.96 × SEM)

Note:
For small samples (typically n < 30), use a value from the Student’s t-distribution instead of 1.96.

For other confidence levels (e.g. 90% or 99%), use a different multiplier (e.g. 1.645 for 90%, 2.58 for 99%).

Example:
A study finds the average systolic blood pressure in a sample is 130 mmHg, with a SEM of 2 mmHg.
→ 95% CI = 130 ± (1.96 × 2) = 126.1 to 133.9 mmHg

This means we are 95% confident that the true population mean lies between 126.1 and 133.9 mmHg.

Important distinction:
  • Standard deviation (SD) measures the variation between individual data points in a sample.
  • Standard error (SE or SEM) measures the precision of a sample estimate (like a mean).

Therefore SD is unaffected by sample size whilst SEM decreases as sample size increases.
